name: Cosmic coincidence problem
summary: Dark energy density rho_DE ~ rho_matter today, even though they evolve very differently with redshift (rho_matter ~ a^-3, rho_DE ~ const for Lambda). Through almost all of cosmic history the two densities differ by many orders of magnitude; only a narrow window around z ~ 0 has rho_DE / rho_matter ~ O(1). 'Why now' is the puzzle: the observer-relevant epoch is the unique one where the two are comparable.

characteristic_numbers.expected: anywhere from 10^120 to 10^-120 across cosmic history
characteristic_numbers.note: Today's coincidence Omega_DE ~ Omega_matter is unique to a narrow z window.

characteristic_numbers.expected: no SM relation between dark and baryonic densities
characteristic_numbers.note: Sub-puzzle: dark and baryonic matter densities are also within a factor of six despite (presumably) very different origins.

principles_stressed.note: 'Why now' requires either (a) a tracker / scaling solution where DE tracks matter (quintessence with appropriate potential, k-essence), (b) anthropic selection where observers can only emerge in the era when structure forms, or (c) some deeper dynamical link between the two sectors. None is established.

history: Articulated explicitly in the late 1990s after the 1998 acceleration discovery. Steinhardt (1997, 'Quintessential cosmology') framed the problem; Carroll (2001), 'The cosmological constant', Living Reviews in Relativity 4, 1, gives a comprehensive review. Tracker quintessence (Zlatev-Wang-Steinhardt 1999) and anthropic arguments (Weinberg 1987) are the leading proposed resolutions.

references.citation: Carroll, 'The cosmological constant', Living Reviews in Relativity 4, 1 (2001)
Zlatev, Wang, Steinhardt, 'Quintessence, cosmic coincidence, and the cosmological constant', Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 896 (1999)
Weinberg, 'Anthropic bound on the cosmological constant', Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 2607 (1987)
